channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: An Artist Renounced His Family. They Sued to Acquire His Life’s Work.
item.description: A settlement is reached in the case of Mike Disfarmer, who renounced his family. Decades later they sued to take back his life’s work. When heirs battle the people who built their legacies, the art may be at stake.
item.dc:creator: Arthur Lubow

item.title: Through Violent Memes on Social Media, Trump Promotes the War on Iran
item.description: A series of White House social-media videos turn the carnage in Iran into gleeful entertainment.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: A video released by the White House
item.media:description: A social media video released by the White House mixed pop culture clips with bombing footage.

item.title: How ‘The Sopranos’ Came to Life
item.description: A new exhibit at the Museum of the Moving Image in Queens traces how the creators developed the look and themes of the show.
item.dc:creator: Sara Aridi

item.title: To Pay the Bills, a Cult Band Becomes a Doors Tribute Act
item.description: With even critically acclaimed bands struggling to make a living, the British group Field Music has come up with a novel way to top up its income.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Joanna Yee
item.media:credit: Joanna Yee for The New York Times

item.title: At This Festival, the Dead of Winter Is the Life of the Party
item.description: In the Hudson Valley town of Chatham, N.Y., a weeklong event called “The Dark” took over theaters, bars, a church, a bookshop, a library and even a sauna.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Gilbertson
item.media:description: In the work by Schneider, above, an unseen narrator guides the audience through a matrix of 4,000 LED lights.

item.title: Minecraft’s Uncensored Library Adds a United States Wing
item.description: The Uncensored Library, a digital project that chronicles attacks on journalistic liberty, is adding a U.S. wing alongside those dedicated to Russia, Saudi Arabia and others.
item.dc:creator: Will Bahr

item.title: Quentin Tarantino Plans ‘Swashbuckling’ Play for London’s West End
item.description: “The Popinjay Cavalier,” written and directed by Tarantino, will open next year. It is described as a “rambunctious comedy of deception” set in 1830s Europe.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Lorenzo Franzoni/Getty Images
item.media:description: Quentin Tarantino’s foray into theater comes several years after the release of his ninth feature film, “Once Upon a Time … in Hollywood,” in 2019. He has long hinted that he will retire after making 10 films.

item.title: Suspect in Rihanna Shooting Is Charged With Attempted Murder
item.description: A woman fired a semiautomatic weapon at Rihanna’s home while she was there with ASAP Rocky and their three children on Sunday, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Charly Triballeau/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Nobody was injured in the shooting at Rihanna’s Los Angeles mansion on Sunday.

item.title: Michelle Pfeiffer Makes It Look Easy. It Isn’t.
item.description: At 67, the actress is still reinventing herself, with two new TV shows, “The Madison” and “Margo’s Got Money Troubles,” and an evolving attitude about her work.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski and Amy Harrity
item.media:credit: Amy Harrity for The New York Times

item.title: Fed Up With High Costs, American Theater Takes a Trip to London
item.description: Skyrocketing budgets and falling profitability have driven a new form of offshoring, with U.S. producers staging shows across the Atlantic.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Joshua Atkins for The New York Times
item.media:description: David Hunter as F. Scott Fitzgerald in a rehearsal for the new American musical “Beautiful Little Fool,” which had an initial run this winter at the Southwark Playhouse in London.

item.title: Casting, Which Will Be Celebrated at This Year’s Oscars, Has Changed Drastically
item.description: What once involved an actor and a casting director in a room is now a technologically advanced exercise with pros and cons for performers.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman

item.title: Review: A New ‘Tristan und Isolde’ at the Metropolitan Opera
item.description: Yuval Sharon’s debut production featured an astonishing performance by the soprano Lise Davidsen. It is the event of the season.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Michael Spyres, left, and Lise Davidsen in the tunnel as the title lovers in “Tristan und Isolde” at the Metropolitan Opera.

item.title: Bill Kurtis to Leave NPR’s ‘Wait Wait … Don’t Tell Me!’
item.description: As the irreverent quiz show’s judge and scorekeeper, Kurtis, a former news anchor known for his rich voice, has helped find comedy in current events since 2014.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Daniel Boczarski/Getty Images
item.media:description: Bill Kurtis’s introductions on “Wait Wait … Don’t Tell Me!” have included “I’m the voice so creamy, you better take some Lactaid.”

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Addresses the Notion of a Drone Strike on California
item.description: “Isn’t this how ‘Ironman 3’ started?” Kimmel said after the F.B.I. warned state officials tobb prepare for a retaliatory Iranian drone strike on the West Coast ahead of Sunday’s Academy Awards.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: Top-End Auction Sales Help Pull Global Art Market Out of Slump, Study Says
item.description: The top drivers included a $2.2 billion auction week in New York and strong fall fairs, according to the annual Art Basel and UBS report.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Art Basel Paris fair at the Grand Palais last October.

item.title: Haitian President’s Widow Recalls Killers Spoke Spanish and Used Nicknames
item.description: The prosecution used testimony by Martine Moïse about the language she heard on the night her husband was killed to support its claim that the assassination was carried out by a hired Colombian hit squad.
item.dc:creator: David C. Adams
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Martine Moïse, left, and her son in Port-au-Prince, Haiti, in 2021, after the attack that killed her husband.

item.title: Stephen Koch, 84, Dies; Champion of a Belatedly Hailed Photographer
item.description: A writer and critic, Mr. Koch struggled for years to shepherd his friend Peter Hujar’s underappreciated, Bohemian-world artwork to posthumous glory.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter

item.title: Peter Schneider Dies at 85; His Novels Explored a Divided Germany
item.description: His best-known work, “The Wall Jumper,” proved prescient in its contention that the country would remain split even after reunification.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Ulf Andersen/Getty Images
item.media:description: Peter Schneider in 1994. He was considered one of the most important literary figures of his generation, in part because his life was so intertwined with the postwar German experience.

item.title: Angelika Saleh, the Angelika of Angelika Film Center, Dies at 90
item.description: After making the journey from prewar Germany to Madison Avenue opulence, she gave her name to one of New York’s most influential indie cinemas.
item.dc:creator: Alex Traub

item.title: In Japan, Making Wooden Kokeshi Dolls
item.description: A Japanese artisan demonstrates the skill needed to produce kokeshi.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Morelli and Andrew Faulk
item.media:description: The family of Teruyuki Hiraga has been making kokeshi, or traditional Japanese wooden dolls, for eight generations.

item.title: Dan Simmons, Genre-Leaping Author of ‘The Terror,’ Dies at 77
item.description: He moved easily and prolifically through science fiction, fantasy, horror, thrillers, crime and historical fiction. His book “The Terror” was made into a cable TV series.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:credit: Sophie Bassouls/Sygma, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Dan Simmons in 1996. “He said from the beginning that he wanted to write what he wanted to write,” his wife, Karen Simmons, said, “and if the publisher didn’t want him to go in that direction, he’d switch publishers.”

item.title: Jessie Buckley Became a Mother for ‘Hamnet.’ Then She Became a Real One. (Encore)
item.description: The Oscar-nominated actress talks about the primal love and grief of motherhood.
item.dc:creator: Anna Martin, Reva Goldberg, Emily Lang, Davis Land, Christina Djossa, Amy Pearl, Sara Curtis, Elisa Gutierrez, Jen Poyant, Lynn Levy, Daniel Ramirez and Dan Powell
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by The New York Times; Background: Brian Rea; Inset photo: Chris Pizzello/Invision, via Associated Press

item.title: Late Night Makes Fun of Pete Hegseth’s Fruit Baskets
item.description: The Pentagon reportedly spent lavishly on doughnuts, ice cream machines and stickers — “and despite all that, nobody came to Pete Hegseth’s birthday party,” Stephen Colbert said.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: How ‘Sync Music’ Became the Soundtrack to Our Lives
item.description: “Sync music” has become the soundtrack to our lives — whether we realize it or not.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Francis Bradley
item.media:credit: Emily Shur for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kara Talve (left) and Anze Rozman, who are married to each other, collaborating on a composition at Bleeding Fingers studios in Santa Monica, Calif.

item.title: Harry Styles and 8 More Boy Band Stars Who Went Solo
item.description: As the former One Direction singer releases his fourth album, listen back to how Bobby Brown, Ricky Martin and others made their own statements.
item.media:credit: Gareth Cattermole, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Harry Styles onstage at the Brit Awards earlier this month.

item.title: Dozens of States Weigh Continuing Live Nation Antitrust Case
item.description: After the Justice Department reached a settlement with the concert giant, the judge overseeing the trial asked the states and the company to do the same.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Mario Tama/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Justice Department, joined by 39 states and the District of Columbia, sued Live Nation in May 2024, arguing that the company was a monopoly that dominated the music industry.

item.title: ‘Scarpetta’ Review: Nicole Kidman Stars in Long-Awaited Forensics Drama
item.description: Nicole Kidman stars in Prime Video’s adaptation of Patricia Cornwell’s long-running series of Kay Scarpetta novels.
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale
item.media:credit: Connie Chornuk/Amazon Prime Video
item.media:description: “Scarpetta” stars Nicole Kidman (with Bobby Cannavale) as the title character, a chief medical examiner.

item.title: New York Philharmonic Announces 2026-27 Season
item.description: A new era for the orchestra begins in September, when Gustavo Dudamel officially becomes its music and artistic director.
item.media:credit: James Estrin/The New York Times
item.media:description: Gustavo Dudamel, who will officially become the New York Philharmonic’s music and artistic director in the 2026-27 season.

item.title: Alma Allen Signs With Mega-Gallery After Venice Biennale Fallout
item.description: Alma Allen has joined Perrotin, months after two other galleries dropped him over his selection to represent the United States at the “art world Olympics.”
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Jake Naughton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alma Allen at his home in Mexico City. The Utah-born sculptor, who lives and works in Mexico, will represent the United States at the 2026 Venice Biennale.

item.title: At 100, György Kurtág Is Still Searching for the Right Note
item.description: György Kurtág, the eminent composer and Hungarian national treasure, has reached a rare milestone yet shows little interest in retirement.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Akos Stiller for The New York Times
item.media:description: The composer György Kurtág in 2018. He recently celebrated his 100th birthday in Budapest.

item.title: ‘Fukushima: A Nuclear Nightmare’ Review: An Emotional Choice
item.description: This documentary offers a rundown of the 2011 nuclear meltdown with special attention to the shift workers who risked their lives while trying to stabilize the plant.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: How the Oscars Best Picture Winner Is Chosen
item.description: Best picture is the only Oscar category decided by preferential ballot. Our reporter Marc Tracy explains how that works.
item.dc:creator: Marc Tracy, Léo Hamelin and Laura Salaberry

item.title: Book Review: ‘Kids, Wait Till You Hear This! A Memoir,’ by Liza Minnelli
item.description: “Kids, Wait Till You Hear This!” is a familiar reminder that growing up in showbiz can lead to awards and adulation, but also to heartache.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.category: Kids, Wait Till You Hear This! (Book)
item.media:credit: Steve Schapiro/Corbis, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Liza Minnelli in 1972, the year she starred as Sally Bowles in “Cabaret.”

item.title: Late Night Scolds Trump Over Gasoline Prices
item.description: “You can lie about many things in American life, but one thing you can’t lie about is gas prices,” Late Night host Seth Meyers said. “Everyone sees it.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)

item.title: Monti Rock III, Gleefully Untalented ‘Tonight Show’ Favorite, Dies at 86
item.description: He couldn’t sing, dance or tell funny stories. But Johnny Carson loved him and his persona: a D-list star clinging to celebrity.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.media:credit: Ron Galella/Ron Galella Collection, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Monti Rock III in 1975. He was a flamboyant dresser, a transparently gay man in the subversive style known as camp in the mid-60s.

item.title: ‘Let’s Go for a Walk’: Three Dance Artists Strip Down Movement
item.description: At the Dance Reflections festival, Nacera Belaza, Anne Teresa De Keersmaeker and Noé Soulier all attempted some form of going back to basics. Results varied.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert
item.category: Skirball Center for the Performing Arts

item.title: Greg Greeley, Former Amazon Executive, to Lead Simon & Schuster
item.description: Greg Greeley, who once ran Amazon’s books and media business, will succeed Jonathan Karp as chief executive at one of the largest book publishers in the U.S.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:credit: Johannes Simon/Getty Images
item.media:description: Greg Greeley, whose 19-year tenure at Amazon included a stint running its books and media business, has succeeded Jonathan Karp as chief executive of Simon & Schuster.

item.title: After Kennedy Center Exit, Washington National Opera Returns
item.description: Washington National Opera managed to resume performances within two months of its abrupt departure. But there are still challenges ahead.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Elman Studio
item.media:description: Washington National Opera’s new production of Scott Joplin’s “Treemonisha,” which opened at Lisner Auditorium at George Washington University on Saturday.

item.title: Review: Jealousy, Deception and Another Round of ‘Othello’
item.description: American Ballet Theater opened a short spring season at Lincoln Center with a full-length work by Lar Lubovitch from 1997. Time hasn’t freshened it up.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas

item.title: Review: Harry Styles Hits the Club on ‘Kiss All the Time. Disco, Occasionally.’
item.description: The pop star’s new album revels in the communal experience of clubbing. But his gleaming songs don’t reveal much about the man behind them.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.category: Kiss All the Time. Disco, Occasionally. (Album)
item.media:credit: Gareth Cattermole/Getty Images
item.media:description: “Kiss All the Time. Disco, Occasionally.” is the fourth solo album by the former One Direction member Harry Styles.

item.title: Live Nation, Ticketmaster’s Owner, Settles Antitrust Case With Justice Dept.
item.description: The agreement, which requires a judicial sign-off, would avoid a breakup of the concert giant. But states that joined the suit object to the terms.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario, David McCabe and Olivia Bensimon
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The tentative settlement ends a case that had bipartisan political support in Washington.

item.title: ‘Testament of Ann Lee’ and the Entwining of Dance and God
item.description: “The Testament of Ann Lee” and Shaker traditions represent how movement has been a constantly evolving conduit to heaven.
item.dc:creator: Margaret Fuhrer

item.title: Don’t Fence Ted McGinley In
item.description: In “Shrinking,” this veteran performer has finally found a job in which he feels fully appreciated. “It’s the greatest experience I’ve had in my acting career,” he said.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski
item.media:credit: Ryan Pfluger for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ted McGinley said his role in “Shrinking” on Apple TV represents “a second chance.”

item.title: For Longtime Kennedy Center Patrons, a ‘Lifeline’ Has Been Cut
item.description: As President Trump prepares to close Washington’s premier performing arts venue for two years, loyal patrons wonder where they’ll get their cultural fix.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Since the Kennedy Center opened in 1971, some three million people have relied on it for concerts, plays and other cultural offerings each year, according to the Government Accountability Office.

item.title: ‘Scarpetta,’ Plus 7 Things to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: A new show staring Nicole Kidman premieres, and the 98th Academy Awards air live.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.category: The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Connie Chornuk/Prime
item.media:description: Nicole Kidman and Simon Baker in “Scarpetta.”

item.title: After a Shooting at Rihanna’s Home, a Woman Is Held on Suspicion of Attempted Murder
item.description: No injuries were reported after the shooting on Sunday. A 35-year-old woman is being held on roughly $10 million bail, jail records show.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens and Christina Morales
item.media:credit: Mike Coppola/Getty Images For The Gotham Film
item.media:description: Rihanna has three children with her partner, ASAP Rocky.

item.title: Country Joe McDonald, Whose Antiwar Song Became an Anthem, Dies at 84
item.description: One of the starring acts at Woodstock, he and his band, the Fish, came out of the Bay Area’s psychedelic rock scene. He went on to a long career as a solo artist.
item.dc:creator: Jim Farber
item.category: Country Joe and the Fish (Music Group)
item.media:credit: United Archives, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Country Joe McDonald in 1981. The tone of the politics and social commentary in his songs could range from whimsical to snarky.

item.title: On ‘S.N.L.’, Pete Hegseth Says Iran ‘Isn’t a War, It’s a Situationship’
item.description: Colin Jost added to his airtime playing Hegseth, the defense secretary, in the opening of a “Saturday Night Live” broadcast hosted by Ryan Gosling.
item.dc:creator: Dave Itzkoff
item.media:credit: NBC Universal, via YouTube
item.media:description: The longtime Weekend Update co-anchor Colin Jost reappeared in his own recurring role as the defense secretary, Pete Hegseth.

item.title: ‘New Michelangelos’ and the ‘Dan Brown Approach’ to Art History
item.description: Works newly attributed to the Renaissance artist had exciting stories behind them. But experts say they are unlikely to be by his hand.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal
item.media:credit: Ilvy Njiokiktjien for The New York Times
item.media:description: Two Brussels collectors bought a painting at auction in Italy that they say is a lost Michelangelo.

item.title: ‘Rooster’ Review: Steve Carell Goes to School
item.description: In HBO’s new Sunday night comedy, the star of “The Office” plays a best-selling novelist caught up in campus politics.
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale

item.title: In a Screen-Dazzled World, a Theater Critic Has the Antidote
item.description: The Times’s new chief theater critic is taking up the mantle as the industry moves over rocky ground.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:credit: Michelle V. Agins/The New York Times
item.media:description: Helen Shaw, The Times’s new chief theater critic, outside the Walter Kerr Theater last week.

item.title: MoMath Brings Prime Numbers to a Prime New Location
item.description: After outgrowing its original home, the National Museum of Mathematics has added new exhibits and an art gallery space in what was an empty storefront along the Avenue of the Americas in Manhattan.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang and Emon Hassan

item.title: The Met Opera’s Desperate Hunt for Money
item.description: The Met has looked to a foreign government, to new strategies, even to outer space, in its scramble to find money to sustain the country’s largest performing arts organization.
item.dc:creator: Graham Bowley and Julia Jacobs

item.title: Tatjana Wood, Award-Winning Comic Book Colorist, Dies at 99
item.description: She was part of the acclaimed creative teams on comic book series for DC Comics, including Swamp Thing, which she called “Shvampy” in her German accent.
item.dc:creator: George Gene Gustines

item.title: When DOGE Unleashed ChatGPT on the Humanities
item.description: Documents show how A.I. was used to cancel most previously approved grants by the National Endowment for the Humanities as the agency embraced President Trump’s agenda.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: The headquarters of the National Endowment for the Humanities in Washington. The agency was created in 1965 to promote a wide appreciation for the humanities and “mutual respect for the diverse beliefs and values of all persons and groups.”

item.title: Thaddeus Mosley, Sculptor Who Found Fame in His Last Decade, Dies at 99
item.description: A self-taught artist, he turned reclaimed wood into striking abstract works influenced by Brancusi, Noguchi and African art.
item.dc:creator: Will Heinrich
item.media:credit: Ross Mantle for The New York Times
item.media:description: Thaddeus Mosley at his studio in Pittsburgh in 2023. Of the racial and economic barriers he faced, he said: “I knew that I wasn’t going to get the same recognition, have the same opportunity. But I tried, because it’s something I wanted to do.”

item.title: Conductor Andris Nelsons Has Become a Cautionary Tale
item.description: Andris Nelsons’s abrupt departure from the Boston Symphony Orchestra shouldn’t be surprising to those who have witnessed his artistic decline.
item.dc:creator: David Allen

item.title: I Make Connections. Here’s What I’m Actually Thinking.
item.description: The 1,000th Connections puzzle is out today. Wyna Liu, the writer behind the game, knows you have thoughts.
item.dc:creator: Wyna Liu

item.title: Sturgill Simpson, Olivia Rodrigo: 8 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: Sturgill Simpson’s political screed, Olivia Rodrigo’s Magnetic Fields cover and the Lunar New Year song burning up the charts in Vietnam.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Frazer Harrison/Getty Images for Stagecoach
item.media:description: Sturgill Simpson released his new album as Johnny Blue Skies & the Dark Clouds on YouTube.

item.title: Bill Lawrence’s TV Empire Is Built on Humor and Heart
item.description: Bill Lawrence, the man behind comedies-with-heart like “Scrubs” and “Ted Lasso,” is in the midst of a career renaissance. He has five shows on the air now, including “Rooster” with Steve Carell.
item.dc:creator: Jeremy Egner

item.title: A Washington Museum Zeros In on the Watergate Scandal
item.description: The Watergate museum, now in a pop-up phase, focuses on the political crime that brought down Nixon.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Nazaryan

item.title: Book Review: ‘Stories,’ by Helen Garner
item.description: A newly released collection of the Australian master’s short fiction shows her sympathy, her virtuosity and her ear.
item.dc:creator: Joumana Khatib
item.media:credit: Darren James/Orion Books
item.media:description: One of Australia’s most acclaimed writers, Garner creates work that is visceral, incandescent and unmistakably her own.
